title: Slater-Usoh-Steed (SUS) Presence Questionnaire
description: 6-item presence questionnaire for virtual environments. One of the shortest validated instruments. Based on Draper et al. (1998) notion of presence as a mental state. Originally appeared in Slater, Steed & Usoh (1993); current 6-item version from Usoh et al. (2000).
citation: Usoh, M., Catena, E., Arman, S., & Slater, M. (2000). Using presence questionnaires in reality. Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments, 9(5), 497-503.
response_format: 7-point scale (1 = Not at all to 7 = Very much)

reliability.note: Reliability not formally reported by original authors. Correlates strongly with PQ (r=0.51).

No_subscales.description: The SUS is treated as a unidimensional scale — all 6 items contribute to a single presence score

items.text: To what extent did you feel you visited a real place?
How often did you feel you were in the virtual environment?
How often did real-world events intrude on your virtual experience?
How much did the virtual environment feel like real life?
How much did you feel that the virtual environment was more real than the real world?
How real did the virtual world seem to you?
items.anchor_high: As real as the real world

scoring.method: Mean of all 6 items after reverse-scoring SUS3; or median for ordinal interpretation
scoring.note: Skarbez et al. (2017) recommend SUS for direct, short presence measurement. Correlates with PQ (r=0.51). Negatively related to task errors.

intended_use: Virtual environments — any immersive VR system
theoretical_basis: Presence as a mental state; suspension of disbelief; 'being there' as primary construct
known_limitations: No subscales — cannot decompose components of presence
Reliability not formally validated by authors
Does not address social presence, engagement, or negative effects
distinguishing_features: Recommended for quick direct presence measurement (Skarbez et al., 2017)
Widely used across VR research communities
